items.title: Fifth Third official: AI will help banks 'out-code' vendors
items.summary: Jay Plum, head of consumer lending at Fifth Third Bank, says artificial intelligence is fundamentally shifting relationships between banks and their third-party software vendors, allowing banks to do things on their own that they would previously rely on vendors to do for them, like identify risky loans and prepare for exams.
items.editor_reason: Fifth Third lending head shares hands-on practitioner perspective on AI enabling banks to 'out-code' vendors - real implementation experience from someone who's actually built in banking.

items.title: Big AI spenders are reaping large productivity gains
items.summary: American Banker data found that those who upped artificial intelligence spending by more than 25% in the last year saw major improvements in employee output.
items.editor_reason: American Banker data on AI spending and productivity gains provides concrete evidence from practitioners who've actually deployed AI at scale.

items.title: Is AI already conscious?
items.summary: Why it’s difficult and perhaps even morally perilous to rule out the possibility of AI consciousness- by Aeon VideoWatch on Aeon
items.editor_reason: Aeon video examining AI consciousness through philosophical lens - exactly the kind of serious epistemological engagement with LLMs that connects technical and humanities thinking.

items.title: From Governance Norms to Enforceable Controls: A Layered Translation Method for Runtime Guardrails in Agentic AI
items.summary:
arXiv:2604.05229v1 Announce Type: new 
Abstract: Agentic AI systems plan, use tools, maintain state, and produce multi-step trajectories with external effects. Those properties create a governance problem that differs materially from single-turn generative AI: important risks emerge dur- ing execution, not only at model development or deployment time. Governance standards such as ISO/IEC 42001, ISO/IEC 23894, ISO/IEC 42005, ISO/IEC 5338, ISO/IEC 38507, and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework a
items.editor_reason: ArXiv paper on translating governance norms to enforceable controls for agentic AI - directly addresses the auditability and risk management challenges in enterprise AI.

items.title: Anthropic's Project Glasswing - restricting Claude Mythos to security researchers - sounds necessary to me
items.summary:
Anthropic didn't release their latest model, Claude Mythos (system card PDF), today. They have instead made it available to a very restricted set of preview partners under their newly announced Project Glasswing.
The model is a general purpose model, similar to Claude Opus 4.6, but Anthropic claim that its cyber-security research abilities are strong enough that they need to give the software industry as a whole time to prepare.

Mythos Preview has already found thousands of high-severity vulner
items.editor_reason: Simon Willison's analysis of Anthropic's restricted Claude Mythos release under Project Glasswing - practitioner perspective on AI safety in production systems.

items.title: Eight years of wanting, three months of building with AI
items.summary:
Eight years of wanting, three months of building with AI
Lalit Maganti provides one of my favorite pieces of long-form writing on agentic engineering I've seen in ages.
They spent eight years thinking about and then three months building syntaqlite, which they describe as "high-fidelity devtools that SQLite deserves".
The goal was to provide fast, robust and comprehensive linting and verifying tools for SQLite, suitable for use in language servers and other development tools - a parser, formatte
items.editor_reason: Detailed account of building with AI tools over three months by someone who actually shipped code - real practitioner experience with agentic development workflows.

items.title: Vulnerability Research Is Cooked
items.summary:
Vulnerability Research Is Cooked
Thomas Ptacek's take on the sudden and enormous impact the latest frontier models are having on the field of vulnerability research.

Within the next few months, coding agents will drastically alter both the practice and the economics of exploit development. Frontier model improvement won’t be a slow burn, but rather a step function. Substantial amounts of high-impact vulnerability research (maybe even most of it) will happen simply by pointing an agent at a sour
items.editor_reason: Thomas Ptacek's analysis of AI's impact on vulnerability research - authoritative practitioner voice on how frontier models are reshaping security work.

items.title: The IDE Is Dead. Long Live the ADE
items.summary: Why I told my engineers to delete their IDEs, what happened next, and where software development is actually heading.
items.editor_reason: Engineering leader's account of replacing IDEs with Agent Development Environments - real implementation experience of AI-transformed development workflows.

notes: Strong showing from Simon Willison's curated technical content and ArXiv philosophical AI papers
Several items touch the intersection of AI governance, security, and enterprise implementation
Notable gap: fewer cross-pillar items than usual, suggesting sources are becoming more specialized
